answers.field_label: Why Are You Interested In Serving On The Pennsylvania Prek Counts Head Start Supplemental Assistance Program Advisory Committee
answers.value:
I am interested in serving on the Pennsylvania PreK Counts/Head Start Supplemental Assistance Program Advisory Committee because early childhood education has been at the center of both my professional life and my personal life for the past 15 years, and I want to help shape policy and implementation at a statewide level rather than only within my own program.

Professionally, I currently serve as the Early Childhood Program Coordinator for the Carlisle Area School District in Cumberland County. In this role I oversee our PA Pre-K Counts classrooms, collaborate with our local Head Start partner, and work directly with families whose children have a wide range of needs: from children experiencing homelessness and food insecurity to children with IEPs and dual-language learners. I see, on a daily basis, where state guidance is strong and supportive and where it can be confusing, inconsistent, or difficult to implement in a way that truly centers children and families. Serving on this committee would allow me to bring that ground-level perspective forward so that the policies and guidance we craft are realistic, equitable, and responsive to what actually happens in classrooms and homes.

I also have a personal connection to these programs. My younger son, James, attended a PA Pre-K Counts program from 2019–2021, and my niece is currently enrolled in a Head Start classroom in Dauphin County. As a parent and an aunt, I have seen how high-quality Pre-K transformed our children’s readiness for kindergarten—academically, socially, and emotionally. At the same time, I’ve also experienced the stress of navigating waitlists, transportation challenges, and the stigma that some families still feel when they hear the term “Head Start.” These experiences have convinced me that family voice must be centered in our decision-making, and I believe I can represent both the educator and family perspectives in a balanced, honest way.

Another reason I am eager to serve is that Pennsylvania is at a critical moment in terms of workforce stability and program sustainability. Our teachers and family child care providers are exhausted and underpaid, and we are seeing classrooms close even while demand from families grows. In my current position I have had to make painful decisions about reducing hours and combining classrooms because of staffing shortages and funding constraints. I want to work with others on this committee to explore creative solutions around workforce pipelines, compensation, and professional development that honor early childhood educators as the skilled professionals they are.

I also bring experience working with diverse communities. Our district serves a growing number of immigrant and refugee families, particularly from Latin America and the Middle East. I have helped develop family engagement strategies that are linguistically and culturally responsive, including evening orientation sessions with interpreters, bilingual newsletters, and partnerships with local community organizations. I would like to ensure that statewide guidance and funding structures explicitly support this kind of inclusive practice and do not unintentionally create barriers for families who are new to the country, speak languages other than English, or do not have reliable transportation or internet access.

Finally, I believe in data-informed decision making that never loses sight of the human stories behind the numbers. I have experience collecting and analyzing child outcome data, attendance trends, and family survey responses, and I am comfortable asking hard questions about what the data really tell us. But I also know that the most meaningful insights often come from listening carefully to a parent who is juggling three jobs or to a teacher who is trying to support a classroom of children while also navigating their own family’s challenges. On this committee, I would commit to lifting up both the quantitative and qualitative evidence so that our recommendations are grounded in reality and guided by a clear vision: that every 3- and 4-year-old in Pennsylvania, regardless of ZIP code, race, language, or family income, can access a high-quality early learning experience that sets them up for long-term success.

In short, I am interested in serving because I care deeply about this work, I understand the systems from multiple angles (teacher, administrator, and parent), and I am ready to contribute the time, energy, and thoughtfulness required to strengthen Pre-K Counts and Head Start Supplemental Assistance across the Commonwealth.

answers.field_label: Practical Experiences In Other Setting That Is Not Listed Please Describe
answers.value:
In addition to my work in school district and Head Start partner sites, I have several other relevant practical experiences:

1. Community-Based Preschool Collaboration: From 2017–2020, I served as the early childhood liaison for a collaboration between our district and a community-based nonprofit preschool housed in a church basement in Carlisle. While not formally licensed as a child care center at the time we began, they were transitioning toward licensure and quality improvement. I worked alongside their director to align their curriculum with PA Early Learning Standards, develop a shared transition-to-kindergarten protocol, and coordinate screening and referrals for children who needed early intervention. This setting was unique because it blended tuition-based slots, scholarship slots funded by local donors, and eventually several PA Pre-K Counts slots as we braided funding. Navigating the complexity of licensing, quality standards, and multiple funding streams in a nontraditional space helped me appreciate the challenges smaller community programs face.

2. Home Visiting and Play-and-Learn Groups: For three years I partnered with our county’s Parents as Teachers program to co-facilitate monthly "play-and-learn" groups held at the public library and in a housing authority community room. These groups served families with children ages birth to five, many of whom were on waiting lists for Pre-K Counts or Head Start. While not a formal classroom setting, these playgroups functioned as a bridge into more structured early learning programs. I helped design developmentally appropriate activities, modeled positive adult-child interactions, and provided informal coaching to parents about school readiness. This experience gave me insight into the needs of families who are not yet connected to a formal program.

3. Faith-Based and Evening Child Care Programs: Earlier in my career, I volunteered at an evening child care program run by a local church for families attending recovery meetings. The program operated from 6:00–9:00 p.m. twice a week and served a very mixed-age group of children, from toddlers through school-age. Although not a typical early childhood classroom, we incorporated many early learning elements—story time, simple math games, and social-emotional skill-building—while also being responsive to the trauma and instability many of the children were experiencing. This experience reinforced the importance of flexible, trauma-informed approaches and helped me understand how early learning can and should happen in nontraditional hours and spaces.

These varied experiences across community-based, faith-based, and informal group settings have deepened my understanding of how families actually access care and education, and how state policies can either support or unintentionally hinder these creative, grassroots solutions.

answers.field_label: Non Profit Or Other Statewide Committee Experience Please Describe
answers.value:
I have served in several committee and advisory roles that I believe are relevant to this application:

1. Pennsylvania Early Learning Council – Regional Workgroup (South Central): From 2020 to 2023, I participated in the South Central regional workgroup of the PA Early Learning Council as a district representative. In this role, I contributed to discussions on Pre-K expansion priorities, workforce challenges, and strategies for improving coordination between school districts, Head Start, and child care providers. I helped review draft guidance documents related to transition to kindergarten and provided feedback on how proposed policies would play out in a mixed-delivery system.

2. United Way of Carlisle & Cumberland County – Education Impact Committee: Since 2018, I have served on the United Way’s Education Impact Committee, which reviews grant proposals from early childhood and youth-serving organizations and makes funding recommendations to the Board. I have helped develop scoring rubrics that prioritize equity, evidence-based practices, and strong family engagement. This experience has given me a broader view of how local nonprofits are innovating to meet family needs and how small shifts in funding criteria can encourage better alignment with state priorities like Pre-K Counts and HSSAP.

3. Local Interagency Coordinating Council (LICC) for Early Intervention: I have been a member of our county’s LICC since 2016, representing the school district’s early childhood programs. The council brings together representatives from early intervention, health services, child welfare, and early learning programs to coordinate services for young children with developmental delays and disabilities. I have co-chaired a subcommittee focused on smoothing transitions from early intervention (Part C and Part B/619) into preschool and kindergarten, which has involved aligning timelines, sharing data responsibly, and improving communication with families.

4. School District Equity and Inclusion Committee: Within the Carlisle Area School District, I sit on the districtwide Equity and Inclusion Committee, where I advocate for early childhood to be fully integrated into equity conversations, not treated as an afterthought. This has involved reviewing discipline data for our Pre-K classrooms, examining enrollment patterns by race and income, and recommending changes to our outreach strategies and policies (for example, revising enrollment materials to be more accessible and less stigmatizing for families living in poverty).

Collectively, these committee experiences have strengthened my ability to collaborate across agencies, interpret policy, and translate big-picture goals into practical steps. They have also given me experience in consensus-building, facilitation, and ensuring that family and provider voices are present in decision-making spaces.

answers.field_label: Other Please Describe
answers.value:
Other relevant attributes and experiences include:

• Bilingual and Culturally Responsive Practice: I am conversational in Spanish (intermediate level) and have completed multiple trainings on working with multilingual learners and their families. While I still rely on professional interpreters for complex conversations, my language skills help me build initial rapport with Spanish-speaking parents and ensure that they feel welcomed and respected in our programs.

• Trauma-Informed and Resilience-Focused Approach: I completed a 40-hour training in trauma-informed care through the Pennsylvania Child Welfare Resource Center in 2019 and have since integrated those principles into our Pre-K classrooms. This has included revising our behavior guidance policies, introducing calming corners, and training staff on recognizing signs of trauma and secondary trauma. I bring this lens to all discussions about policy and practice, with a focus on preventing re-traumatization and supporting both children and staff.

• Data and Continuous Quality Improvement Skills: I am comfortable working with data from Teaching Strategies GOLD, Work Sampling, attendance systems, and family surveys. I regularly lead our team through Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) cycles to pilot changes—such as adjusting arrival routines or modifying our family communication methods—and then use data to determine whether those changes are effective.

• Relationship-Building and Communication: Colleagues often describe me as someone who can "speak both languages"—the language of the classroom teacher and the language of administrators and policymakers. I am able to translate policy language into practical steps for educators and, conversely, bring teachers’ and families’ concerns back to leadership in a way that is constructive and solution-oriented.

• Commitment to Equity and Inclusion: I am particularly focused on ensuring that children of color, children experiencing poverty, and children with disabilities are not left behind in our early childhood systems. In my own program, I advocated for and helped implement a weighted lottery for our Pre-K Counts slots that gives priority to children experiencing homelessness, children in foster care, and children with significant risk factors. I would bring that same equity-focused mindset to the Advisory Committee’s work.

Overall, I bring a blend of hands-on classroom experience, administrative leadership, family perspective, and systems-level committee work that I believe would add value to the Pennsylvania PKC/HSSAP Advisory Committee.
